LO 41B

Head & Neck Anatomy · Block 5

The Palate

Anatomy & histology of the hard and soft palate; distribution, function, and innervation of the muscles controlling the soft palate

Overview Hard Palate Soft Palate Muscles Vessels Nerves Histology Quiz

Overview

GROSS ANATOMY

The palate forms a shelf dividing the nasal cavity (above) from the oral cavity (below). It plays a critical role in separating the aerodigestive system and protecting the airway from aspiration.

Hard Palate — Anterior ²/₃
Bony shelf composed of horizontal plates of the maxilla and palatine bone. Forms the floor of the nasal cavity and the roof of the mouth.
Soft Palate — Posterior ¹/₃
Flexible muscular-aponeurotic structure composed of a thin dense connective tissue palatine aponeurosis with attached skeletal muscles.
Inferior view of the oral cavity showing the hard palate, soft palate, and surrounding structures.
Lateral midface view showing the hard palate (ant. 2/3) and soft palate (post. 1/3) dividing the nasal and oral cavities.

Hard Palate

BONY SHELF
Bony Composition
RegionBone / ProcessNotes
Anterior 2/3Palatine process of maxillaCarries low transverse ridges on inferior surface for food grinding; faint median raphe marks fusion seam of the two palatine processes
Posterior 1/3Horizontal process of palatine boneCarries the greater and lesser palatine foramina posterolaterally
Hard palate is flanked on anterior and lateral sides by the superior alveolar ridge.
Key Foramina
ForamenLocationContents (Artery / Nerve)
Incisive (incisor) foramenAnteriorly on midlineNasopalatine nerve; septal branch of superior labial artery
Greater palatine foramenPosterolateral on horizontal processGreater palatine artery & nerve → turns anteriorly to supply hard palate
Lesser palatine foramenPosterolateral, just posterior to greaterLesser palatine artery & nerve → turns posteriorly to supply soft palate
⚡ Clinical Pearl — Cleft Palate
Incomplete fusion between the two palatine processes of the maxilla during development leads to cleft palate. The median raphe marks the normal fusion seam.

Soft Palate

MUSCULAR-APONEUROTIC
  • Palatine aponeurosis — dense fibrous connective tissue core; muscles attach to it
  • Laterally, the aponeurosis blends with the aponeurotic walls of the pharynx
  • Posterior margin is free, slopes downward into the oropharyngeal isthmus, bearing the uvula
Palatine Folds (Arches)
Two folds of dense connective tissue + muscle descend from the lateral soft palate:
  • Palatoglossal fold (anterior pillar) — contains palatoglossus muscle; reaches the base of tongue
  • Palatopharyngeal fold (posterior pillar) — contains palatopharyngeus muscle; embeds into the lateral wall of pharynx
Aponeurosis Attachments
Two muscles insert on the superior surface of the palatine aponeurosis:
  • Tensor veli palatini — attaches laterally (via pterygoid hamulus)
  • Levator veli palatini — attaches vertically (elevates the palate)

Muscles of the Soft Palate

5 MUSCLES
Muscle Origin Insertion Action Innervation
Levator veli palatini Petrous temporal; auditory tube cartilage Palatine aponeurosis (superior surface — vertical) Elevates soft palate; opens auditory tube during swallowing CN X (vagus) via pharyngeal plexus
Tensor veli palatini Scaphoid fossa of sphenoid; auditory tube cartilage Wraps around pterygoid hamulus → palatine aponeurosis (lateral) Tenses soft palate; opens auditory tube CN V₃ (mandibular) — medial pterygoid nerve
Palatoglossus Palatine aponeurosis Lateral tongue (blends with transverse tongue mm.) Elevates tongue; narrows oropharyngeal isthmus; depresses soft palate CN X via pharyngeal plexus
Palatopharyngeus Palatine aponeurosis Posterior border of thyroid cartilage; pharyngeal wall Elevates pharynx/larynx; closes nasopharynx; depresses soft palate CN X via pharyngeal plexus
Musculus uvulae Posterior nasal spine; palatine aponeurosis Mucosa of uvula Shortens & elevates uvula; contributes to velopharyngeal closure CN X via pharyngeal plexus
⚡ Key Fact — Innervation Rule
4 of 5 soft palate muscles are innervated by CN X (vagus) via the pharyngeal plexus.
Tensor veli palatini is the exception — it is the only palatal muscle innervated by CN V₃ (medial pterygoid nerve).
Clinical Relevance — Velopharyngeal Function
During swallowing and speech, the soft palate elevates (levator veli palatini) and tenses (tensor veli palatini) to seal the nasopharynx from the oropharynx — preventing nasal regurgitation. Loss of CN X → palate deviation toward the intact side; uvula deviates away from the lesion.
Muscles of the soft palate (lateral view). Shows tensor veli palatini, levator veli palatini, palatoglossus, palatopharyngeus, and pharyngeal constrictors.
Ventral view of palate showing greater/lesser palatine arteries, nasopalatine nerve, greater/lesser palatine nerves, and tensor palati muscle.

Blood Supply

VASCULATURE
VesselForamenDirectionTerritory
Greater palatine a. Greater palatine foramen Turns anteriorly Inferior surface of hard palate
Lesser palatine a. Lesser palatine foramen Turns posteriorly Inferior surface of soft palate
Septal br. of superior labial a. Incisive (incisor) foramen Passes superiorly Anterior nasal septum
Memory Trick
Greater → anterior (hard palate). Lesser → posterior (soft palate). Both emerge from the pterygopalatine fossa via the palatine canal, which is a branch of the maxillary artery.

Innervation

SENSORY + MOTOR
Sensory Innervation (CN V₂ — Maxillary)
NerveForamenDirectionTerritory
Greater palatine n. Greater palatine foramen Turns anteriorly Inferior surface of hard palate (general sensation)
Lesser palatine n. Lesser palatine foramen Turns posteriorly Inferior surface of soft palate
Nasopalatine n. Incisive foramen (from nasal septum) Descends inferiorly Anterior underside of palate (behind incisors)
Motor Innervation (Muscles)
Pharyngeal Plexus (CN X dominant)
The pharyngeal plexus (CN IX + CN X + sympathetic fibers) supplies motor innervation to all soft palate muscles except tensor veli palatini. The primary motor fibers for palatine muscles travel via the vagus (CN X).
  • CN X (pharyngeal plexus) → levator veli palatini, palatoglossus, palatopharyngeus, musculus uvulae
  • CN V₃ medial pterygoid n. → tensor veli palatini (only exception)

Microanatomy of the Palate

HISTOLOGY
Hard Palate — Layer by Layer
SurfaceEpitheliumLamina PropriaSubmucosa / Deep
Oral side Keratinized stratified squamous epithelium Fibrous lamina propria strongly bound to periosteum; dermal pegs extend outward into epithelium; midline raphe has thin LP fused directly to periosteum Laterally: submucosa with fatty tissue + mucous glands between LP and periosteum; transverse ridges in anterior hard palate — LP fused directly to periosteum at crests
Central core Periosteum and bone
Nasal side Respiratory epithelium (pseudostratified ciliated columnar + goblet cells) Lamina propria containing sero-mucous glands and deep layer of elastic fibers —
Fetal palate histology. Upper: overview with nasal (respiratory epithelium) and oral (stratified squamous) sides, submucosa, and developing bone. Lower: nasal mucosa with respiratory epithelium, lamina propria fused to periosteum.
Full fetal palate section showing nasal cavities (superior), hard palate bone core, palate cavity, and soft palate with upper lip.
Soft Palate — Layer by Layer
SurfaceEpitheliumLamina PropriaSubmucosa / Core
Oral side Non-keratinized stratified squamous epithelium (can be very thick; penetrated by long papillae from LP — similar to dermal papillae of thick skin) Dense fibrous LP with deep border of elastic fibers Numerous large mucus glands in submucosa
Central core Bands of skeletal muscle embedded in the palatine aponeurosis; mucous glands scattered between muscle bundles
Nasal side Respiratory epithelium (pseudostratified ciliated columnar + goblet cells) — transitions to stratified squamous at posterior border Thin lamina propria attached to underlying palatal aponeurosis; scattered small mucus glands; no submucosa —
Full soft palate section (L→R: anterior to posterior). Nasal side (top): respiratory epithelium + lamina propria on palatal aponeurosis. Oral side (bottom): non-keratinized SSE + LP. Center: mucous glands in submucosa + skeletal muscle.
Left: non-keratinized stratified squamous epithelium of soft palate oral mucosa with LP papillae. Center: mucous acini in submucosa (nuclei compressed basally by mucus globules). Right: nasal surface showing respiratory epithelium → palatine aponeurosis → mucous glands deep.
Uvula
Structure
A squamous epithelial bag suspended from the posterior margin of the soft palate, containing:
  • Mucous glands
  • Bundles of skeletal muscle (musculus uvulae)
Left: magnified nasal mucosa of soft palate — pseudostratified ciliated columnar epithelium + goblet cells on LP + palatal aponeurosis beneath. Right: skeletal muscle in soft palate (cross- and longitudinal sections) — could be tensor, levator, palatoglossus, or palatopharyngeus.
Top: labeled muscles of the palate. Bottom: fetal palate histological section with developing bone, nasal cavities, and upper lip.
Quick Comparison: Oral vs. Nasal Surface
FeatureHard Palate — OralHard Palate — NasalSoft Palate — OralSoft Palate — Nasal
EpitheliumKeratinized SSERespiratory (PSCCE)Non-keratinized SSERespiratory (PSCCE) → SSE posteriorly
Glands in LPNone (anterior)Sero-mucous glandsNoneScattered small mucus glands
SubmucosaLateral: fatty + mucous glandsNoneLarge mucous glandsAbsent
LP attachmentFirmly to periosteumFirmly to periosteumDense fibrousTo palatine aponeurosis
Elastic fibers—Deep in LPDeep border of LP—
⚡ Histology Buzzwords for Exams
  • Hard palate oral: keratinized SSE + dermal pegs + no submucosa at raphe/ridges
  • Hard palate nasal: respiratory epi + sero-mucous glands + elastic fibers deep
  • Soft palate oral: non-keratinized SSE + large submucosal mucous glands + elastic fibers at LP base
  • Soft palate nasal: PSCCE → SSE at posterior edge; no submucosa; thin LP on aponeurosis
  • Uvula: squamous bag + mucous glands + skeletal muscle (musculus uvulae)

🧠 Self-Quiz — LO 41B

1. The hard palate is formed by which two bones / processes?

Anterior 2/3: palatine process of the maxilla. Posterior 1/3: horizontal process of the palatine bone.

2. Which nerve is the ONLY palatal muscle NOT innervated by the pharyngeal plexus (CN X)?

Tensor veli palatini — innervated by the medial pterygoid nerve from CN V₃.

3. What type of epithelium lines the ORAL surface of the hard palate vs. soft palate?

Hard palate oral: keratinized stratified squamous epithelium.
Soft palate oral: non-keratinized stratified squamous epithelium.

4. What is the direction of the greater palatine artery/nerve after exiting the greater palatine foramen, and what does it supply?

Turns anteriorly → supplies the inferior surface of the hard palate.

5. A patient cannot open their auditory tube when swallowing. Which TWO muscles are likely involved, and what are their respective innervations?

Levator veli palatini (CN X via pharyngeal plexus) and tensor veli palatini (CN V₃). Both open the auditory tube during swallowing — dysfunction → Eustachian tube dysfunction, otitis media.

6. Describe the central core of the soft palate histologically.

Bands of skeletal muscle embedded in the palatine aponeurosis, with mucous glands scattered between muscle bundles.

7. What are the two palatine arches, and what muscle does each contain?

Anterior arch (palatoglossal fold) — contains palatoglossus; reaches base of tongue.
Posterior arch (palatopharyngeal fold) — contains palatopharyngeus; embeds into lateral pharyngeal wall.

8. What is the nasal surface epithelium of the SOFT palate, and what is unique about its posterior border?

Mostly respiratory epithelium (pseudostratified ciliated columnar + goblet cells), but transitions to stratified squamous epithelium at the posterior border. The lamina propria is thin, attached to the palatal aponeurosis, and there is no submucosa.
